topic: Sharp partial identification of the conditional average treatment effect when the propensity score is bounded away from {0,1} but the outcome regression admits heavy tails (no uniform sub-Gaussian / sub-exponential moment bound). Find a regime-opening or strict-extension result on the bound width and its dependence on a heavy-tail index alpha.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp CATE partial-identification bounds under strict propensity overlap and regularly varying outcome tails, with endpoint width expressed explicitly as a function of the tail index alpha.

source_refs.claim: Frames Manski-style treatment-effect endpoints as support-dependent and notes that credible support bounds are often unavailable when outcomes are heavy-tailed.
source_refs.author: Grace Lordan and Kaveh Salehzadeh Nobari

source_refs.claim: Claims sharp partial identification of conditional causal effects without bounded outcomes using propensity-score-derived f-divergence bounds, but not alpha-indexed width laws.
source_refs.author: Yonghan Jung and Bogyeong Kang

source_refs.excerpt: bounded f-divergence between the misspecified and true propensity and a bounded sup-norm gap

open_problems.why_unsolved: Published tail-uncertainty work identifies the support-dependence of Manski-style endpoints, and newer information-theoretic bounds remove bounded-outcome assumptions abstractly through divergence duality. The missing regime is a population sharp-width characterization for conditional effects in which strict overlap controls treatment weights but regular variation or a finite-alpha moment condition controls how much unobserved tail mass can move the mean. The closest CausalSmith banked proposal used bounded sup-norm outcome-regression misspecification, so it did not enter the heavy-tail regime.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp endpoint theorem for tau(x) over all conditional laws satisfying e(x) in [epsilon,1-epsilon], observed conditional treated/control laws, and a tail restriction such as P(|Y| > t | A = a, X = x) = L_a(t, x)t^{-alpha}, with a closed-form or convex-program width W_alpha(epsilon, x).

open_problems.open_problem: Establish the alpha phase transition for informativeness of CATE bounds: finite nontrivial width, infinite but sign-informative width, or Manski-vacuous width.

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp likelihood-ratio bounds for treatment-effect generalization and highlights the unbounded-outcome vacuity problem that calls for tail or moment restrictions.
source_refs.author: Amir Asiaee, Samhita Pal, Cole Beck, and Jared D. Huling

source_refs.excerpt: the ATE is identified by a sharp non-trivial interval whose width scales as O(1/sigma_min)

open_problems.why_unsolved: The outcome-shift literature makes clear that unbounded outcomes can make likelihood-ratio worst-case means vacuous, but it leaves the tail or moment restrictions as an open modeling layer. The prior proximal proposal supplies a phase-transition template for point, interval, and trivial regimes, yet its obstruction is an operator singular value rather than tail mass. No existing source classifies the CATE identified set by alpha when overlap is fixed.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient phase theorem giving critical alpha or moment-budget thresholds for finite CATE width, sign-only information, and vacuity, together with extremal conditional distributions witnessing sharpness at each boundary.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop sharp marginal-sensitivity CATE bounds when the adversarial treatment odds are bounded covariate-by-covariate and the conditional outcome laws have only finite alpha moments.

source_refs.claim: Provides sharp functional interval estimators for CATE under unobserved confounding using adversarial weights.
source_refs.author: Nathan Kallus, Xiaojie Mao, and Angela Zhou

source_refs.claim: Gives tight CATE and ATE bounds under a Gamma selection-bias condition, plus orthogonal ATE bound estimation.
source_refs.author: Steve Yadlowsky, Hongseok Namkoong, Sanjay Basu, John Duchi, and Lu Tian

open_problems.why_unsolved: Sharp CATE sensitivity papers solve the adversarial weighting problem under bounded odds-ratio style confounding, but the role of heavy-tailed outcomes is not isolated as an identified-set width parameter. The banked QTE proposal has the right covariate-conditional odds-ratio envelope but targets quantile curves, not mean endpoints under finite-alpha tail budgets. The unresolved obstacle is the interaction between bounded adversarial weights and tail allocation in the conditional mean objective.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A marginal-sensitivity CATE endpoint formula or dual program whose optimizer is characterized under finite alpha moments or regular variation, showing exactly how adversarial weights place mass in the treated and control tails and how the resulting width depends on alpha and Gamma.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend bounded selection-probability mean bounds to CATE with unbounded outcomes by replacing fixed support bounds with tail-index constraints.

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp population-mean bounds when sample-selection probabilities are unknown but lie in a specified interval.
source_refs.author: Peter M. Aronow and Donald K. K. Lee

source_refs.claim: Shows that auxiliary shape constraints such as symmetry or log-concavity can tighten sharp population-mean bounds under unknown selection probabilities.
source_refs.author: Luke W. Miratrix, Stefan Wager, and Jose R. Zubizarreta

source_refs.excerpt: characterizing the tightest sharp interval as a function of the discontinuity-magnitude

open_problems.why_unsolved: Unknown selection probabilities and propensity-overlap uncertainty are formally close: both produce weighted mean objectives over partially observed or counterfactual outcome distributions. Existing sharp mean bounds become extreme-outcome driven unless support or strong shape restrictions are imposed. The prior RDD manipulation proposal uses a cap-envelope/trimming structure with bounded manipulation, but it does not translate finite-alpha tails into the cap that determines conditional mean width.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp weighted-mean or trimming theorem for selection probabilities in [pi_min, pi_max] and conditional tails with index alpha, reducing to Aronow-Lee under bounded support and yielding CATE width formulas after conditioning on X = x.

open_problems.open_problem: Quantify how limited information pooling trades off overlap robustness and heavy-tail robustness in treatment-effect bounds.

source_refs.claim: Constructs bounds between Manski bounds and inverse-propensity weighting that are robust when conditioning variables are numerous or overlap is violated.
source_refs.author: Sokbae Lee and Martin Weidner

open_problems.why_unsolved: Pooling limited information across observations addresses overlap and high-dimensional conditioning, but the bound construction is not organized around heavy-tailed outcomes. When outcome tails dominate endpoint instability, the optimal amount of pooling may depend on alpha as well as the overlap constant; that two-way tradeoff is not characterized in the existing pooling-to-IPW continuum.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An optimal-pooling theorem minimizing identified-set width plus estimation error over pooling neighborhoods under finite-alpha moments, with explicit dependence on epsilon, alpha, and covariate-cell geometry.

open_problems.open_problem: Build valid inference for sample-constrained partial-identification endpoints when both the objective and feasible constraints are estimated from heavy-tailed samples.

source_refs.claim: Frames many partial-identification problems as optimal values over feasible sets where both the objective and constraints are empirically estimated.
source_refs.author: Matthew J. Tudball, Jack Bowden, Rachael A. Hughes, and Kate Tilling

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp ATE sensitivity bounds and estimators with double sharpness and double validity, but the stated target is ATE rather than heavy-tail CATE endpoint inference.
source_refs.author: Jacob Dorn, Kevin Guo, and Nathan Kallus
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: Sample-constrained partial identification supplies the right stochastic-optimization viewpoint for bound endpoints, and double-valid sensitivity inference supplies a robustness goal. Neither source resolves the empirical-process failure caused by no sub-Gaussian or sub-exponential outcome envelope. Heavy tails can affect both the estimated objective and the estimated feasibility region, so endpoint confidence sets need alpha-dependent robustification rather than classical uniform convergence alone.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-moment stochastic-program inference theorem for CATE bound endpoints using robust empirical objectives and constraints, with coverage and width rates stated as functions of alpha.

open_problems.open_problem: Give a closed-form primal-dual characterization of CATE bounds under bounded propensity and alpha-moment outcome restrictions, with strict tightening relative to support-only bounds.

source_refs.excerpt: PRTE bounds strictly tighter... by a computable amount equal to the moment-cone width

open_problems.why_unsolved: The banked MTE proposal has a moment-cone-width and dual-certificate template for partial identification, but it concerns multi-instrument MTE and PRTE rather than unbounded potential-outcome means under overlap. For heavy-tail CATE, the missing piece is an explicit cone or dual norm that encodes alpha-moment restrictions and yields sharp conditional mean endpoints rather than finite-support LP bounds.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A primal sharp identified interval and matching dual width formula for tau(x), where conditional alpha-moment constraints form the feasible cone and the dual certificate proves strict tightening over support-only or Manski-style bounds.

literature_map:
Manski (1990, AER) anchors worst-case treatment-effect bounds; bounded outcome support directly controls width.
Aronow and Lee (2013, Biometrika) recast bounded unknown selection probabilities as sharp mean intervals, close to propensity-bounded weighting.
Miratrix, Wager, and Zubizarreta (2018, Biometrika) tighten selection bounds using shape restrictions; heavy-tail shape classes remain outside that template.
Masten and Poirier (2018, Econometrica) index departures from conditional independence through conditional treatment-assignment probabilities.
Kallus, Mao, and Zhou (2019, AISTATS) provide sharp CATE intervals under unobserved confounding via adversarial weights.
Yadlowsky, Namkoong, Basu, Duchi, and Tian (2022, Annals of Statistics) give scalable CATE/ATE bounds and orthogonal ATE inference under bounded odds-ratio confounding.
Tudball, Bowden, Hughes, and Tilling (2023, Biometrika) frame sample-constrained partial identification as estimated stochastic optimization.
Dorn, Guo, and Kallus (2025, JASA) add double-valid/double-sharp robustness for ATE sensitivity bounds.
Lee and Weidner (2021, arXiv/cemmap) span Manski bounds to IPW through limited pooling, relevant to poor overlap but not alpha-heavy tails.
Asiaee, Pal, Beck, and Huling (2026, CLeaR/PMLR) derive sharp LR outcome-shift bounds and highlight unbounded-outcome vacuity.
Lordan and Salehzadeh Nobari (2026, SSRN) is the closest tail-uncertainty source, but it targets confidence bands for interval ATEs rather than alpha-indexed sharp CATE width.
Jung and Kang (2026, CLeaR/PMLR) claim unbounded-outcome conditional causal bounds from propensity-derived f-divergences, but alpha-specific width laws are not supplied.
Overall tension: overlap controls weights, but without sub-Gaussian/sub-exponential outcomes the identified-set width is governed by tail allocation; the literature does not yet give a clean sharp W_alpha(epsilon, x).
prior_proposal_map:
Target qid pid_heavy_tail_cate_bounds/v1 had no proposal or reviewer JSON yet; only init/state pipeline records were present.
Bank mining used pid_offline_policy_joint_misspec_v1 as the closest bounded-propensity plus outcome-regression misspecification template.
Bank mining used eid_proximal_phase_v1 for a point/interval/Manski phase-transition template, but its obstruction is proximal completeness rather than alpha tails.
Bank mining used pid_qte_marginal_sensitivity_v1 for covariate-by-covariate odds-ratio sensitivity, but the target there was quantile envelopes.
Bank mining used pid_rdd_manipulation_bounded_v1 for sharp cap/trimming interval structure, not heavy-tail mean bounds.
Bank mining used pid_mte_multi_iv_v1 for a primal-dual moment-cone-width template.
No prior proposal found an explicit heavy-tail index alpha for CATE bound width or combined overlap bounded away from 0/1 with unbounded outcome regression.
Study-pipeline gaps were inspected as augmenting context; most LATE and interval-regression gaps were off-topic, while the partially verified Manski nonparametric-bounds gaps reinforced the intersection/inference background but were not used as direct output anchors.
